title: Orange marmalade
subtitle: Classic bitter orange preserve
excerpt: Whole oranges and a lemon simmered until soft, then sliced and boiled with sugar to a glossy, intensely flavoured marmalade with a pleasant bitter edge. The definitive British preserve. Makes approximately five 340 g jars.
sourceNotes: British marmalade has been made since the 18th century, with Seville oranges (in season January, February) being the traditional choice for their high pectin and bitter flavour. The whole-fruit method of simmering the oranges before cutting produces a more even texture than cold-cut methods. Documented by Delia Smith in Complete Cookery Course (1978), by Jane Grigson in Fruit (1982), and by Pam Corbin in River Cottage Handbook No. 2: Preserves (2008).

content.text: The whole-fruit method is the most reliable way to make marmalade at home. The oranges are simmered whole until the skins are completely soft, this takes about an hour. Once cool enough to handle, you cut them, remove the pips, and shred the peel to the thickness you prefer. The pips and pith go back into the pan in a muslin bag while the marmalade boils: they are very high in pectin and help the set.

items.name: Orange
items.prepNote: Seville if available (Jan, Feb), otherwise navel

content.text: Place {{orange}} whole oranges and {{lemon}} lemon in a large saucepan. Cover with 2 litres of water. Bring to the boil, cover, and simmer for 1 hour until the skin is completely soft when pierced. Remove the fruit and leave to cool. Reserve the cooking water. Once cool enough to handle, halve the oranges and lemon. Scoop out the flesh, pips, and pith into a square of muslin and tie into a bag. Shred the peel to your preferred thickness. Sterilise five jars at 120°C for 15 minutes. Put a plate in the freezer. Return the cooking water to the pan. Add the shredded peel, the muslin bag, and the juice from the flesh. Add {{granulated-sugar}} of sugar and stir over low heat until dissolved. Bring to a rolling boil. Boil hard for 15 to 20 minutes, testing on the cold plate after 15. When the marmalade wrinkles when pushed, remove from the heat. Rest 10 minutes before potting, this prevents the peel from floating. Remove the muslin bag. Pour into the sterilised jars and seal immediately.

items.fix: Squeeze the muslin bag hard against the side of the pan before removing to release maximum pectin. Return to the boil for 5 more minutes and test again.
items.cause: Not boiled hard enough, or muslin bag not used to release pectin from the pith and pips.

items.fix: The skin should offer no resistance when pierced with a knife after the initial hour. If still tough, simmer for another 30 minutes before proceeding.
items.cause: Orange skin was not simmered long enough in the first stage.

items.fix: Rest the finished marmalade for 10 minutes, stirring gently, before potting. This thickens the set slightly and suspends the peel.
items.cause: Poured into jars too soon after boiling.
items.symptom: Peel floating to the top of the jars

content.text: Seville oranges are in season for about six weeks in January and February. They are very bitter and high in pectin, which is why marmalade made with them has a pronounced bitter edge and sets reliably. Navel and other sweet oranges make a milder, less bitter marmalade that still sets well. Both are correct; the preference is personal. If you want the classic British bitter marmalade, use Seville in January.
